speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewee
text: Gates open up. I see this German military. And I said, what is this? On the left, there was a little office. All the Americans were in there, in the little office, because they were prisoners, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went in there, I shouted like, hey, [NON-ENGLISH], are you mad? What-- I couldn't say-- I spoke German and Czech and Russian. Didn't understood me. Suddenly a man comes in with the ambulance. And they say to him, you speak German, don't you? And he comes in and says, [NON-ENGLISH]. I said [NON-ENGLISH]. I said to him, you know, you see that number for where I come from? I said, and they're going to put me in prison with those bastards here, with those SS and these military? I'm-- He said, what do you want me to do? I said, please, please get me out of here. Let-- let me go with you. In the middle of the street, wherever you go, put me off. I find my own way. But not in here.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. I didn't-- I went with him. He put me off in a little village, and I went on. I knocked at a door in Czhechoslovakia-- I knocked on the first door, and there was a couple. And I said to them, look-- I could speak Czech a bit, you know. I want to stay the night here, I haven't got nothing to eat. I showed my number. They-- they put me in there. They gave me their own bed to sleep in, first the food. They kissed me. The next day, they said, you stay here with us. I said, no, no, no. I have to move on. So I moved on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went, happy on the street. I went towards Pilsen. And there was the American army. But I got into a little town for-- and suddenly, I was picked up by the police. I don't know how that happened. I-- I was walking loose, like a little [NON-ENGLISH], not maybe for bad.  He wanted to know what I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was moving about. I didn't want to go anywhere. I wanted to be free.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Czech police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Czech--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Czech. So they take me to prison. And they put me into a basement, in the dark prison. And suddenly, the gates-- I said what's this? What do I suffer?

speaker: interviewer
text: Suddenly the gate opens up. A tall man comes in. He was a man from the-- from the trains, a train chief, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me out. And he said come. He said come, come with me. [NON-ENGLISH], come. I'll take you to my home, and I'll talk you to a place called Susice. And there's also somebody who was in Prague. And there are some people they are living there, Jewish people. And I'll put you with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I wasn't-- I was so-- what's it called? So untrusting. They took me to their house. He took me to his house. He was with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They received me there, so marvelous. I was eating. And when I was eating, I was already organizing. There was bread there, I put into my pocket, meat I put into my pocket. I was thinking, the next day I've got to have food. And they were looking at me. They were looking at me. The only thing, food, food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next day I went with them on the train. [SIGHS] I came into Susice. He brought me into [NON-ENGLISH]. And when I walked into the [NON-ENGLISH], there was that big chief from the gendarme sitting there. And he was shouting from far [NON-ENGLISH]! Come here! A Yiddish gendarme. They had Yiddish officers then, in Czechoslovakia. Said, come here. Don't you remember me? [NON-ENGLISH] I was the chief cook from the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Say, yeah. Oh, [NON-ENGLISH], I will put you somewhere here. There's three Yiddish girls-- Polish girls. I took the Nazis out of a house. I gave them the house. And you can live with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he brought me there. Can you imagine? Oy. These ladies-- I don't know where they are now. That happens all in-- these Yiddish are blonde, and the darkish one, they got in there. They saw a Yiddish boy from the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were outside. They looked. They cursed at me. They went mad.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put me into kinder bed there. They wanted to treated me like little child, you know. But I wasn't child anymore. But I wasn't-- what's it called? I accepted it, because I saw different-- but they didn't know it. I was already interested in girls, you know what I mean? So they wanted to play the-- the mothers. So I was standing there and-- the good food, good life.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what happened is, I start touching, and they didn't like it, so we had arguments. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they say, you have to leave. I said, yeah. I'll leave. I said, that this is not possible. The sisters-- they were all sisters. So I said, OK, that won't happen again another-- so what happened? The American army was there. So the American army-- I walked on the street, suddenly I see a Yiddish boy there, in uniform. So I tell him the story. Nice Yiddish girls I live-- but I can't-- he said, you come with me, [NON-ENGLISH]. I said-- come with me. You come to our division. I will make you mascot from the 54th Division Signal Corps.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with them. They gave me American uniform, which I-- and I was with the American army. And I got them girls, because I could speak-- so the first time I went into the division, he said, don't worry. I look after you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Early in the morning they had-- what's it called? When they had the-- in the army you have breakfast. How do  you call it then? Mess-- how do you call that? The mess kitchen, you know? And that opened already-- they said, you can work in that kitchen. So the-- the-- the cook from the army, you know, he said, I've never seen a [NON-ENGLISH] like him. He comes here early, an hour before it opens.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I liked that, you know, the cocktails-- they had that fruit cocktails, and the pancakes, and the syrup. And I was sitting-- he said, he eats, he eats, he eats. But definitely. I mean, I was there to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was with the army, and we understood each other. I start slowly, learning English, slowly because I spoke that slowly English, I looked for them, for girls, in town. I've got photos here of that. And he said, you get-- and I said, I'll go to town.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the first time an American film. In town, they showed the American-- they had picture houses. They showed them with Gary Cooper. I remember at that moment I didn't know-- Then they had the PX. And I also went. I've got my PX, I got my chocolate-- I got everything, you know, like-- I was actually one year, and nobody found. I was happy. I didn't want to go anywhere. I found them girls. I found them this. We played. We-- we-- it was a beautiful life. Free. Free. Until the day came that-- I don't know, a rabbi or somebody said it's not possible that that boy can go-- rabbi in the army. The-- the-- the officer probably, without me knowing, contacted-- and he said this boy can't keep on living with you here. I was in-- in those days, 15 or 16. He has to go learn a profession. He's a very intelligent, bright boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was letter from the rabbi. A rabbi came up. He said, you have to go. You have to-- we send you to Prachatice. And in Prachatice, there is also girls there. And they will help you. They work in the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And a matter of fact, they were the same girls a year later. The whole division, they all saved up money for me. I didn't know what it was, the dollars. They were so in love with me. They gave me that money and I had to go to Prachatice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I was in Prachatice together with the girls, they started-- what's it called? Same girls, it was a year later, I behaved and-- what's it called? But then I-- what's it called? I was again-- they were working for the [NON-ENGLISH], and I was playing about a bit, doing nothing, back from the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day the American army, she was-- the [NON-ENGLISH], She worked for the [NON-ENGLISH], and her friend was an American officer. And they took me one day to the office. And they showed me a photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said to me, do you know this? It was my mother. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I looked at that photo. When she left us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so many years later, my grandparents went there. And I said, this is-- this-- I said, my mummy, this is. They said, you want to go and see her? She lives in London. I said, she lives in London? I spoke English then. We will send you to-- what's it called? To a camp in Munchen-- a displaced person camp, and you will be transported later on to England. So I went to the displaced person camp that was in Dachau. All the children were there. Day by day passed, nothing happens. Week by week pass, nothing happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: After three weeks, I could visit the girls again. And then I had to go back to the camp. I didn't go back to the camp. I went to Nuremberg. I said, I'm going to go to England. I'm not going to wait for the displaced person camp there. I went to officer camp in Nuremberg. I had to have some food, so I went the officer camp. And I stood around there. And there was a terrific captain coming in there, nice chap. And I said to him my number. And I said, I'm hungry.  I've got to have something to eat. [NON-ENGLISH], the Americans were beautiful, you know. The English were harder. The Americans straight away said, I'll take you into-- come. First of all, you come in with me to the officer mess. Oh. Then we eat, he said. I ate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I'm going with you to the-- what's it called?-- he said, captain. If I could find him in America-- it's possible. Then I will get you uniform. You will see that photo here. He put me on the uniform, was much too big for me. Now we go three weeks on holidays. On holiday? Yes, because I have a-- what's it called? A free pass. And we have a hotel here somewhere in Germany where all the officers go. And they took me there. Oh, it was-- the way they looked after me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I put you on-- we go to England. We will go to Dieppe, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and you will go over to New Haven. And I will get you on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: We came there, on the boat. And the English MP said no. And the American's MP said no. And he said, what are you talking about? His mother is in-- We went back. You know what he do? He-- he said to me, Helmut, when they don't look, run onto the boat. Hide yourself under cabin, under where they can't find you. You get to England.

words.text: And he said, what are you

speaker: interviewee
text: I did that. The MPs were running after me. They couldn't run quick enough. I was hiding myself. The boat left.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he said also to me, when you get on the other side, jump the boat. Because jump the boat, I'm on English-- the MPs went with me, with they boat, to take me back. But when you jump the boat, you're on English-- how do you call it? Waters?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: English waters.

speaker: interviewee
text: English waters, grounds. They cannot touch you. I jumped over. It was quite a bit. And the English police came, you know the Bobbies. I didn't know it. And they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, look. I showed my number. I want to see my mother. And what is this here?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me in front of the immigrations. They took all my pockets. They were-- I don't know, stupid people, they wanted to see what I had in my pockets. But I had nothing in my pocket, just a photo for my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, this is here, like a little boy carries about. So they said-- they put me one night into the-- what's it called? Into the quarantine, on the-- to notify my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, my mother was notified. She came. She came. She came on the boat. First thing she said, you shouldn't have done it like this. You should have waited. I said, doesn't matter. You are-- you are suddenly-- I thought my mother was much taller, and suddenly my mother was much smaller. But can imagine, these years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we talked. And then suddenly the policeman came back and he said, the home office has-- they couldn't do any differently, yeah? The home office has agreed that you can stay in England, that you can go with your mother directly. Directly all the newspapermen came onto the boat-- you know, it was New Haven-- and I could go home with her to London. When was the last time you saw her before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1937, '36. But newspapermen came into my home. I didn't want to talk about it then. I said, no, go out, no talk. That's the end of the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: No it's not.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened then, yeah? Life went on. The Jewish kids, I couldn't get along with them, my age.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't know from anything, did they?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You want to know that as well. It was very hard then. It was very hard. I kept it in me, but I was-- I don't know. I wasn't really accepted. All the groups, the boys and-- and-- one way-- a bit like outcast. I don't know. Even my own mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who could understand that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But-- we-- you know, we-- we came out of hell. And we came into a world-- because I was-- when I got to London and I lived with her, I didn't get along-- she lived with a man together. I didn't like him. And I went on the streets everyday in the American uniform. One day the police came up and said to my mother, he can't keep that uniform on. He has to take that uniform and put the civilian clothes, because he's not really a soldier. But I didn't care, so I kept it on. What I'd do the whole day, go to cinemas, learn English. Twice a day, I went to-- I became member of the Maccabee Club. I looked for Jews, I had Jewish friends, Jewish-- but we didn't get along. They didn't accept me. First of all, my mother, we weren't rich. I didn't come from a big family. Concentration camp, [NON-ENGLISH]-- they don't say nothing, don't say nothing. So I didn't say-- But I had a bit of a complex. I always showed my number. I always-- you know, I tell you something. Until now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't have a childhood. Our childhood was taken away from us. We became grown-ups. And we missed that childhood, and freedom. We were searching understanding from people, to give us understanding, to give us positions, to carry us on our feet, high up, like you carry the Torah, and say he has-- we give him everything. No, it was not that. We were a bit stupid. We didn't have university behind us. Everything you see here is self-made, self-learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went through life in England. And also, in England itself, the boys Maccabee Club didn't ask me to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a weight-lifter. I was a bit-- I did a bit sport, but they didn't mix with me. They didn't want to mix.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was like a lone goy. Even my mother being there-- I don't know. I looked-- I looked-- I was lost. Just I was lost. I-- I didn't know where to go. Left, right, went to cinema, went this.

speaker: interviewee
text: My life was empty after the-- until, when I got 18, 19 years, I said, I'm not going to stand it here. I'm going to go in the merchant navy. I want to see the world. So I went into the merchant navy. I saw the world. I went to America. I went there. But in America, they didn't let me in there. I had a stateless passport. I had a lot of difficulties. In England, the English were very bad for the stateless people, with us. The Germans, when they had the passports, they could come back and forth. I used to say to the home office, I said, they killed your soldiers. They killed you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And us, stateless people, you treat like this? But they didn't listen. Young boy, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I had-- you know, people said to me in England, Yiddish people, [NON-ENGLISH]-- don't go to Germany. Don't do that. In '56, I did-- they all have been there before me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I went there, they said to me, you're much too young. [NON-ENGLISH], you're much too young. I said, too young? In German, I said, what are you talking about? When they used to hit us in Birkenau, the death march. You think we didn't feel nothing? We felt it more than somebody grown up. Because we asked, why were we treated like this? We didn't understand it. Children, little children, what have they done bad? And now you tell me we were too young? I told them, you know, said, life until-- I-- I worked-- in the merchant navy, I went around the world. I met a lot of people. I went to Spain. Marvelous thing happened to me in Spain. I was free, you know? I was a steward. And when I was on the boat, I came into Valencia, Gandia, and always sang. There was orchestra. I used to sing songs with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: (SINGING) La da da dee dah, la da dah dah dee dah dah dah. That was a song from Spain. They said the chanteur come. I came back with that boat twice. Little children came on the boat. I gave the m food, because I was a steward. Most beautiful thing happened to me when one day somebody came to cut my haircut. A-- a-- a Spanish-- I spoke a bit of Spanish in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I want to pay him. He said, you don't pay. I said, I don't pay? You gave my son food. Because they were very poor in '56 or '58, the Spanish. That was for me-- what's it called? So I came back to England. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My wife, being in antique business, I met her in England. What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kleist, Elvira Kleist. Not Jewish, I will tell you that. But she was so good. Back to Belgium I came to her parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married later. I was living with her many years together before I got married. I gave her the respect that I gave her my-- she listened to me. She gave me food, steaks like this. In Belgium, they had already after the war, the first country which really had a super life. Their parents were good to me. Suddenly, I found I worked. I became antique dealer, a powerful antique dealer. I made money-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] made good money. I was with the-- there's a Jewish organization in Ghent. We have a little synagogue there. We say Yom Kippur. We say-- I went to Israel. But the only story-- the only story that you hear now, my wife knows. My mother didn't want to know. Don't talk about it, because when we went back to Germany for the restitution, my mother went back. She stayed there-- she's still there, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: I couldn't stay there. I couldn't live there. I had to argue. I said, how can you live there where all your family were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Different people-- the Jewish people who emigrated to every country in the world, England, they're the first ones who came back for the immigration-- for the restitution. Everything was paid to the family. They all had it. The uncle in South America, they all got money for my grandparents' restitution. I didn't want a penny for them. I didn't look for that. But until a year ago, I went many times to Israel. I was over there was the war was won. I had-- you see, if you don't have money, you can't-- Suddenly I hear what-- that all these people are saying, that it's not true. That it's not true-- the lies from Auschwitz. Lies, just lies. It didn't happen, the camps, the right extreme. I start talking. One day, it happened-- very unfortunate it happened. I'll tell you how it happened. It happened one day that the Yiddish organization from Ghent, Bloch, comes to me. I was at-- listen, I had to-- I had to-- I had the business. I had a shop in Brussel. And he said, would you come with me to the embassy in Brussel, the German ambasade-- because the Turks, when they, in [NON-ENGLISH], when they were burned, we want to have a petition. So I said, if you want me to do it, I'm human. I don't like it, either. [NON-ENGLISH], we went there. He couldn't speak German. He came into the German consulate. That's the first time this happens, that I start talking. And two newspapermen, Belgian newspapermen went with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was now in-- two years ago, two, three years. Yes. Date-- or was it before the-- wait a minute. Wait a minute. Yes. No, no. No, no. Something else. I have to go back. No, no. So life went on. This is afterwards. This is in Weimar. I go one day, I come home. I was sitting in front of the television at 10 o'clock. I hear a film, which was made in Holland, But the film was half already gone. And I hear boys talking about my story. And I hear Ernst Sacker. He said, I went to Birkenau and Theresienstadt, and the-- the death march. And I said to my wife, Vira, come. Said, this must be Ernst Sacker! Suddenly, his name comes up, Ernst Sacker. Oh, I say, and the other-- after 55 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next day I telephone the Dutch television. They said to me, is your Helmut Szprycer. The secretary? Oh, she said, we've been looking for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The boy from Holland who wrote the book, he went on a search, and he-- because they said that he was-- that they met in Tel Aviv. They had a gathering and they were talking. What happened to that little-- that [NON-ENGLISH], Helmust Szprycer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next day when I telephone them, I told them that I'm-- yes.  We talked about you. Sherry Dons made the film. So I said, where are they? She gave me the telephone number from the [NON-ENGLISH]. I telephoned them. Helmut, is that you? And I'll-- so sudden-- Los Angeles, and [NON-ENGLISH], I telephoned. He went mad. I went mad.

speaker: interviewee
text: After three weeks I said, I come over. He came over. So suddenly, there was that first experience, that we-- the group from the camp got together.  It's America. That book existed, which I've got here-- that day, what I told you about Bloch, going to the German consul. These two journalist went with us. I gave them the book. Bloch had to-- on the way to Brussels, they had the story. Suddenly they saw this was more interesting, the story, then from the German ambasade, from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And we went in there. I said to the-- the first thing I said to the German is, is it going to start again? In German, [GERMAN]. He said, what do you mean? I said, [GERMAN]. No, no, no. I said, don't tell me. Don't tell me, I said in German. This is not-- this is the-- what's it called? The Turks or the other peoples, the Algerians. But indirectly, you're doing it to the Jews. I said it to him. Will it happen again? I said, how do you-- [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, tell me something. Do you teach in Germany in your school about the Holocaust? Well, in America, they do. But there's nobody who lived it. I said, what are you talking about? So I put up my arm and I showed him my number. The chief ambassador-- he nearly sank in the floor. [GERMAN] I was there. I was there in Birkenau and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not possible, he said. And I think it's time that you do tell the young generation about it. Anyhow, we left the petition there. We went back to Ghent. And then the newspapermen-- it was-- read my story in the book. And the next or two days after he put two pages full, Helmut Szprycer, Birkenau boy, Mengele. The whole papers were full. And that's how the story came out. And then people wrote me letters. People telephoned me. People stopped me on the street. That you suffer like this is unbelievable. And then I started, because the Auschwitz lies, these right extremes, I put them down. So I start telling my story. That's why I'm here also.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why I came also to-- what's it called? I hear it on the television, Walesa is not allowing it. Walesa is wants to do it this way, there's more Poles and this there. There's no kaddish to be said. And I said, what is wrong there? I hear Weisel comes to say kaddish. I said to my wife, I'm going. That's for today, for the anniversary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 50th anniversary--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I said, what do you mean? I'm not going to-- I'm going to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, how are you going to go there? She says, last minute, all hotels are full. I telephoned. They were all full. So I said to the Polish here in the reservation, I said, I'm coming from the 24th to the 25th. Have you got a vacancy then? He said, yes. I book a room there. So my wife said, you-- I said, no, don't worry, once I have a room there, I stay there. They have to sling me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came over. I got the room. I didn't have the invitation. I have nothing, you see. That's why I'm here.  And that's why I'm telling you today that story. It has to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: We had a ceremony today for the 50th anniversary of the liberation of Auschwitz. Yesterday we had a ceremony. It was specifically Jewish ceremony. What did you feel about today? Today's ceremony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yesterday. Yesterday was my anniversary-- not anniversary. My-- how do you call it? [NON-ENGLISH] my-- because I don't-- I don't call it a liberation. The Russians, they didn't liberate nobody. It's a year too late they came. Then there should be 2, 3 million people more in the world. Because these 2,000 they found there, it was like, you know, the rest, they couldn't even use them. They couldn't walk. No liberation for me. But it was as-- yesterday that meeting was from everywhere the Yiddish people came, from all over the world to remember the 6 million, to say kaddish, to give them our blessing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were there, one family, strong in our hands-- and didn't know what's going to happen to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah, of course. You didn't know. And Poland-- anything can happen in Poland. Nobody knew. They could have thrown bombs there. But we did go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that means that we stick together. And today, what happened today-- Tell me a little bit what happened today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, it's what's it called? It's a fantasy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Politically.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? Who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, they want to show the world today. They want to show the world-- kings were there, and diplomats were there. What they come to do there? What did they come to do there? To excuse themself? To say, we are sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Duke of Edinburgh came over here last year, but after 50 years to say it should-- to give them a bit of pleasure for themself before they die that they know they have misdone against us. Is that the way they came there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's the way they came there. But there is no excuse. The whole world, everybody, every person in this world who lived at that moment when that started is guilty what happened to our people there. They're guilty. And now, even the Vatican, these priests, now excuse themself. My sister won't be back. My family won't be back. The rest of my peoples' family won't be back.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they are there-- they are there that excuse themselves. The past has done wrong in those years. Now, now, now, is it? Now? But no, there is no-- the guilt is on them and they can't take it away from us.
